item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really interesting, I like it!

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent change from "negotiate the course" to "navigate the course" was reverted, as it was an unnecessary stylistic change.

Example definition: https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/negotiate
"to deal with (some matter or affair that requires ability for its successful handling)"

Google also gives, "find a way over or through (an obstacle or difficult path)."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite some issues in the writing tone, I think this one is a lot of fun. And I would definitely go for it.

item.title: Re: Rewrite Team - Open
item.content:encoded: This SCP is fine as it is, it does not need a rewrite from someone who believes he "khan doh veterrr".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check addendum 3. The announcer basically chatted up the one guy and then let him leave when it was clear no one else would come.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if one person enters alone? Maybe I missed something, but I don't see anything about that, and it seems like a rather obvious possibility.

item.title: Rewrite Team - Open
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's the sort of change that won't be made to an article. The article is successful without the black boxing, and it's a style thing that different authors will approach differently. It's not a change that would sit well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm honestly not sure if anyone even cares about making an edit like this, but I'm going to throw my hat into the ring anyway. I would like to suggest possibly putting a black box over the date on the Mr. & Mrs. Smith poster, only for the purposes of making it seem more professional. But if no one thinks this idea is worth incoporating, by all means, ignore it. Just throwing something out there for people to think about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does anyone else wish the game show host was Bob Barker?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably not but that does sound awesome

item.title: Favorite SCP
item.content:encoded: This is my all time favorite SCP of the ones I've read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It depends on the author's preference.

Usually sub-designations are used to cover different parts of an SCP that are referred to repeatedly. In this case, I don't think a sub-designation is really necessary, since the studio guardians aren't described in much detail and aren't mentioned often enough to necessitate a sub-designation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just read this for the first time, and have downvoted. This is clearly an early SCP, and was upvoted in the context of the time, but there are a number of ways that it falls below current site standards in my view:

  • all containment procedures must end with explosives of some kind
  • describing the (not hugely interesting) circumstances of discovery before telling the reader what the anomaly is
  • an extremely casual / "story-telling" tone throughout, typified by phrases like:
    • "It is then that the contestants are led to the actual game"
    • "More often than not, the obstacles seen in these games range from incredibly benign to extremely hazardous and life-threatening."
    • "it is not surprising to have the entire pool of contestants succumb to the rigors of the obstacle course"
    • "However, the most mysterious aspect of SCP-024"
    • "Also, more strangely"
  • A surfeit of adverbs/intensifiers throughout.
  • A steadfast refusal to give details of the nature of the obstacle course in order to give the reader the ability to imagine it, meaning a huge reduction in immersion and engagement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any attempts to "break the rules", such as assaulting other contestants and deliberately bypassing obstacles, are met by extreme violence. The announcer will call out the offending contestant, who will be quickly and forcefully ejected from the course by "studio guardians". These studio guardians will immediately materialize within SCP-024 when called upon by the announcer and disappear when not needed. The contestant will never be seen again.

Wouldn't these studio guardians typically be designated as SCP-024-1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also thought of Saw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Object Classes are based on difficulty of containment, not level of danger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't this:

It is at this point the announcer presents the choice of whether to stay or leave SCP-024. Contestants who accept will continue to participate in the game while those who decline are immediately expelled from SCP-024.

Make the Object Class "Safe"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the implication that someone made a sapient game show, then abandoned it when it started becoming too dangerous.
I love game shows (started at age 2 when my fave show was Wheel of Fortune) and I definitely like this SCP! Being the 200th upvote gives me a warm fuzzy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good one, but why is it Euclid? As long as you don’t enter it or just refuse to participate, you’re gonna be fine. So it seems pretty safe to me, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My headcanon: Jigsaw is the announcer with the next Hunger Games taking place here. Contestants including:
-Kondraki and Glass
-Clef and Gears
-Kain and Bright
-Rights and Strelnikov
-Bijhan and Gerald

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
Just noting that a recent change here was reverted. The change in question changed "negotiate" to "navigate", in regards to a course. Seeing as "negotiate" can also mean "find a way over or through (an obstacle or difficult path)", the original wording makes sense.

To preserve author intent, I've reverted the style edit.

As per the site rules and required reading, please don't respond to staff posts if you're not staff. If you have a question regarding this edit, feel free to post a new comment chain or PM a staff member.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the concept and even the execution are interesting (albeit not quite original), the tone is just far too personal for an official report. Not that it matters since this article is in the +160s, but I had to downvote.

On a side note, the idea suggested in this thread that the audience could be made of the game losers seems interesting.

Also, a minor suggestion
Just dropping my 2 cents...

Although suggested changes will probably never see the light of day, I believe this article could benefit from a general description of game stages with progressing hazard levels, for instance:

"During initial stages of the game, contestants are subjected to benign obstacles such as: slow rotating foamed arms, mild pressured water guns, etc. At later stages, obstacles adquire higher hazard levels, instances of such including, but not limited to: swinging steel blades, fully automated machine guns, etc"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As per the Foundation thread on Something Awful, the closing note from an O5 was useless and stupid. With permission from Dexanote, I have removed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually enjoyed this one. It was one of the first I read along with "Bone Hive", 173, and "Demon Born from War"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article was kind of meh for me… But I would like it a thousand times more if the live audience was the losers

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"More often than not, the obstacles seen in these games range from incredibly benign to extremely hazardous and life-threatening."

I would be interested to know what sort of obstacles fall outside of this range.

Wasn't feeling the concept or the execution of this one, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if anyone has checked the videos in the mail box to see if any of the surviving losers appear in the "live studio audience". Maybe that's where they disappear to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of all the articles I've read so far, I'd have to say that this one is my favorite. Very intriguing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I must say I concur. The tone is decided non-clinical.

As well, it seems like there are some rather obvious lapses in the testing protocols.

What happens to anyone who attempts to enter the soundstage when a game is in progress? Are they barred from entry? Invited to watch (stuck as part of the phantom crowd from then on perhaps)?

What do video recording devices carried / planted on the contestants display with regard to the guardians? Or Those carried by winners if wireless transmissions are blocked? What happens after the fact to cameras that are planted? Or hell, can you see in from the doors or a window? Would drilling a hole work? I really don't think the foundation would rely quite so heavily upon just the video evidence being provided to them by an SCP.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Still just a few votes short of breaking into the Top Rated though…
CONGRATULATIONS, YOU'VE WON OUR PRIZE!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Either 682 would tell them it refused to play their disgusting games and be allowed to leave, or it stayed and adapted to the handlers and escaped any way while possibly destroying SCP-024 on the way out.

Also, other question, you can choose to not be a contestant but you're banned from the studio forever afterwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another thought: we should stick 682 in here along with a couple of D-class that really [REDACTED] it off. If it attacks them, that would be considered cheating and it'd get "thrown out"… if it loses, it disappears. If it does manage to win, we're not really any worse off. (So it owns a car or some plane tickets, it's not going to get to use them…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, does everyone who enters have to play? Can four people go in, and one of them choose to be an audience member?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much enjoy the concept, but the writing style seems off. A lot of personality in the writing style, phrases like " Also, more strangely," and "The contestant will never be seen again.", which sounds more like prose than an official report.

Overall, though, very fantastic idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see any need to edit this one. I've always misliked blurring and pixelating images.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, do I need to have permission to change it or should I just go ahead and do it?

You need permission. Ask a mod, admin, or the original author via PM.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, do I need to have permission to change it or should I just go ahead and do it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1st is better. There's no need at all to know whether or not a billboard is there or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://dl.dropbox.com/u/23229414/Rapist%20%231.png

http://dl.dropbox.com/u/23229414/Rapist%20%232.png

Whichever

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Gets glasses)

Fair enough - but the point is still valid. It could use some pixilation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a billboard, broseph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cSc8FWfJQlU

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God damn you all to hell. My childhood. T_T

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It probably just has more obstacles that shoot at you.

it is not surprising to have the entire pool of contestants succumb to the rigors of the obstacle course. If such an event happens, the announcer will express sadness at the lack of a winner and SCP-024 will shut down, resetting only when a new batch of contestants enter.

I'm guessing they know that from the tape, because everyone ELSE in the building is dead.

I'm not feeling it, really. Might have started when I saw the "Mr&Mrs Smith" banner. I liked the movie, but don't we normally blur out things like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My guess is 'no.' Maybe the gun just disappears after he enters, 'confiscated.' Maybe the bouncers are immune to bullets. Maybe the gun just doesn't work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if a D-class armed with a firearm could defend himself against the bouncers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:( I liked Robot Wars…

Anyway, fairly cool.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: And one I dearly wish the local sci fi channel still aired reruns of U_U

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Robot Wars is technically a game show. :-)

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Robots wouldn't work. Have you ever seen robots in a game show? I think not!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't bad, but I think it suffers from 'telling too much'. I think some mild editing to make it a bit more subtle could be useful.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Somehow, I get the feeling the announcer would frown on this. So it should definitely be tried!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whew, I was upset for a little while when I read the part about the announcer being sad if everyone dies, But then I read the part about a recording is put in a mailbox, and that made me relieved.

This article kind of reminds me of Saw, except you get to choose whether or not you want to play the game. Not the most original idea, but a good spin on an old concept. It was entertaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has anyone considered running robots through this, controlled from outside the building?

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Not at all. SCP-024 is supposed to focus on the more physical side of the game show circuit. Whether the announcers are the same person or not though, I'll leave that up to you

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: they do seem quite similar

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any relation to SCP-263? This isn't a criticism - I'm just curious.
